claim_value: Pollinator-friendly, late- and long-flowering perennial wildflower with small white aromatic flowers in heads. Yarrow is mostly sun-loving, but can tolerate some shade, some trampling, and is deer-resistant. The fern-like foliage is partially evergreen so you can enjoy this plant through winter. Yarrow leaves stay soft and green through the winter making it an excellent lawn replacement that can tolerate seasonal mowing but can be vigorous in good garden soil. The rhizomes and roots hold soil making it useful for slopes. It is prized as a cut flower and for its many herbal, edible, and medicinal applications.

claim_value: Seeds have very high viability. Seedlings are fast-growing and have high establishment. This is a great species to try from seed if you are new to seeding or want almost guaranteed success and is a good plant to introduce children to. Yarrow is late-flowering so the seed isn't mature until late summer or into early fall. You can bang the seed heads into a bucket or bag to capture the seed and be prepared to leave them outdoors for many days to observe the insect exodus ! Yarrow can be sown in fall or early spring. When sown in fall, expect ample germination with fall rains, but sometimes also another round of germination into spring. Slugs can be a nuisance with new germinants, but the seed viability is so high with this species that some success is almost always found. These can be started in partial shade or full sun, but if spring sown maintain consistent moisture through their germination and establishment. If started in partial shade, move to full sun as they may become light-deprived. Once they start growing, they're quick to mature and will flower in their first year.

claim_value: A mostly perennial wildflower (sometimes annual) of rich, moist sites in shade to partial shade. Mature plants will produce ample seeds to create lovely patches of small whiteish-pink flowers through most months of the year. Leaves are a deep, dark green and the stems can be a rich wine colour with the colours accenting each other. All parts of the plant are edible but can be bitter during and after flowering. Steaming the leaves can help sweeten the flavour. Always harvest with care from your garden, leaving flowers for the pollinators and seeds for keeping your patch thriving.

claim_value: ḴEXMIN, q’uxmin is gifted to Indigenous Peoples. Please connect with us at info@satinflower.ca. One of our regions striking native carrot family species with globe-shaped flowers on long petioles. This species has a wide range of habitats that it will thrive in, from seasonally wet meadows to coastal sand ecosystems. The foliage is a prized caterpillar-feeding habitat for Anise Swallowtails. Note: This plant is rich in local Indigenous food traditions, we strongly recommend that non-Indigenous people take time to understand and study before using this or other native plants. Please take the time to learn about this plant with the proper resources such as the book Saanich Ethnobotany by Nancy Turner and Richard Hebda. *Edibility depends on the preparation of the plant. Always do your own research prior to consuming plant parts and eat at your own risk.

claim_value: Striking large magenta to pink flowers that bloom later than most other spring annuals. Very drought-tolerant and best suited to very well-drained shallow soils in full sun. As with all native annuals, this plant will spread by seed and form larger patches as long as the area isn't mulched after the seed drops in late summer. The seeds are tiny and must remain near the soil surface where the fall rains coincide with their germination. Through fall and winter, the new leaves will form green carpets (with a hint of pink that match the pink in the flowers!) of small foliage and then go on to bloom the following spring.

claim_value: Coastal Mugwort has the characteristic aroma of the sage species that grow in the interior grasslands and prairies. It grows in full sun to partial shade and appreciates drainage, which is why you often find it growing gravelly areas on the seashore. Coastal Sage is a herb, but gets quite tall and woody, growing almost like a small shrub especially in deeper soil. Eaten raw, the plant is quite bitter, but it can be used as a culinary herb to flavour other foods. This is a great choice for those who might have too many deer grazing their gardens, as deer don't find it palatable. Wind-pollinated.

claim_value: Coastal Mugwort can be sown in fall or early spring. When sown in fall, expect ample germination with fall rains, but sometimes also another round of germination into spring. Slugs can be a nuisance with new germinants. These can be started in partial shade or full sun, but if spring sown maintain consistent moisture through their germination and establishment. Coastal Sage requires well-drained soils or the small plants will rot. If collecting, keep alert for when Coastal Mugwort seed becomes mature as it is subtle. You can bang the browning seed heads into a bucket or bag to capture the seed.

claim_value: A stunning perennial wildflower in the Asparagus plant family with purplish-blue flowers (sometimes light blue blue and occasionally white) and an edible bulb. Yellow, pollen-packed stamens contrast against the colourful tepals and the large, open flowers draw in a rich diversity of pollinators. Common Camas tolerates very dry shallow soils as well as being flooded in winter in clay soils. Plant only in full sun. Note: This plant is rich in local Indigenous food traditions, we strongly recommend that non-Indigenous people take time to understand and study before using this or other native plants. Please take the time to learn about this plant with the proper resources such as the book Saanich Ethnobotany by Nancy Turner and Richard Hebda. *Edibility depends on the preparation of the plant. Always do your own research prior to consuming plant parts and eat at your own risk.

claim_value: A stunning perennial wildflower in the Asparagus plant family with purplish-blue flowers (or sometimes even white) and a large edible* bulb. Yellow, pollen-packed stamens contrast against the colourful tepals and the large, open flowers draw in a rich diversity of pollinators. The bulbs of Great Camas divide readily in deep, rich soils and can be grown in full sun to partial shade. Fall and early winter are ideal seasons to be planting bulbs. Some of our bare bulbs are at a mature, flowering age and can be expected to flower once established, if planted in a suitable location. It can take a few years to establish sufficient roots that can support flowering, and is dependent on soil - Great camas thrives in deep, rich soils. Read more tips about growing Great Camas on this blog post Note: *Edibility depends on the preparation of the plant. Always do your own research prior to consuming plant parts and eat at your own risk. This plant is rich in local Indigenous food traditions, we strongly recommend that non-Indigenous people take time to study and understand the complexities of the species before using or consuming this or other native plants. We recommend learning in a good way first-hand as is possible, or proper resources such as the book Saanich Ethnobotany by Nancy Turner and Richard Hebda.

claim_value: Extremely striking perennial plant with large purple-blue flowers and whorled leaves. Attracts large-bodied bumblebees that can access the flowers effectively. Prefers soils that are relatively wet through winter. The status of this species is currently under debate; some question whether it is native to the area and some confuse it with the garden hybrids that were planted all over the island highway. We continue to regard this entity as native because it occurs regularly throughout our Prairie-Oak Ecoregion, extending south into Washington and Oregon and in similar habitats to which it is found in the Victoria Area; wet clay-rich meadow with winter moisture and relatively deep soils. This entity should be carefully planted and not nearby to garden hybrids with which it may hybridize.

Satinflower reminds us of the richness and uniqueness of our region.

claim_value: This plant has a blue like no other and to name it might be impossible. The flowers are small and the plant is meant to be admired on your knees with the careful eye of a true nature lover. It's an annual species with red seeds and can be grown in full sun to partial shade. In open meadows, plant with well-drained soils or select a rocky outcrop site. As with all native annuals, this plant will spread by seed and form larger patches as long as the area isn't mulched after the seed drops in late spring. In order to germinate, the small seeds need to remain near the soil surface where the fall rains coincide with their germination. Through fall and winter, the new leaves will form blueish-green carpets of small foliage and then go on to bloom the following year in early spring.
notes: Taxonomy review recommended before approval.

claim_value: Small hispid foliage contrasts the light pink flowers that have yellow and white centres. One of the few representatives of the Polemoniaceae (Jacob's Ladder) plant family in the Victoria region. Though the flowers are fairly small, this annual groundcover will attract larger flying bees to shaded sites and help restore groundcover to woodland areas. As with all native annuals, this plant will spread by seed to form larger flowering patches as long as the area isn't mulched after the seed drops in late spring/early summer. In order to germinate, the small seeds need to remain near the soil surface where the fall rains coincide with their germination. Through fall and winter, the new leaves will form fuzzy, green carpets of small foliage and then go on to bloom the following spring.

claim_value: One of the most cheerful and pollinator-friendly meadow plants of Southern Vancouver Island. Small sunflower-like bright yellow flowers provide oodles of colour in mat-forming bunches of blueish-green woolly foliage. The flowers attract an unbelievable abundance of native bees and the plants are very resistant to deer. Woolly Sunflower must be planted in full sun and grows best in well-drained soils. When restoring rocky outcrops, coastal bluffs, or sunny meadows this plant is a must. For home gardeners, try it in a rock garden, raised bed, or even a rock wall where the partially evergreen foliage will hang over the edges and stay green through much of the fall and winter.
